peirce_classification.firstness: Presence of multiple wireless hacking tools and network captures on a laptop
peirce_classification.secondness: Records link the device to Greg Schardt who uses the alias Mr. Evil, and traffic captures of third parties are found
peirce_classification.thirdness: The suspect systematically performs war driving near WiFi hotspots to intercept communications and steal financial credentials

key_iocs.note: Links Greg Schardt to alias Mr.Evil — NIST Q12 answer
mIRC config: user=Mini Me, nick=Mr, anick=mrevilrulez

null_hypothesis: Greg Schardt is a legitimate security professional who analyzes networks as part of his penetration testing work
scoring_notes: Self-validates via published MD5 + community-validated answers from multiple independent forensic writeups. Note: NIST does not publish an official answer key for this case — the CFReDS page (cfreds-archive.nist.gov/Hacking_Case.html) contains only the questions. Community answers have been cross-validated across independent sources but should not be treated as authoritative.
